coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a recent addition to the nanocarbon family, encompassing both crystalline and amorphous phases. They have sparked significant research interest due to their unique electrical and optical properties, remarkable biocompatibility, outstanding mechanical characteristics, customizable surface chemistry, and negligible cytotoxicity. Their current applications are mainly limited to flexible photonic and biomedical devices, but they have also garnered attention for their potential use in intelligent packaging. The conversion of food waste into CDs further contributes to the concept of the circular economy. It provides a comprehensive overview of emerging green technologies, energy-saving reactions, and cost-effective starting materials involved in the synthesis of CDs. It also highlights the unique properties of biomass-derived CDs, focusing on their structural performance, cellular toxicity, and functional characteristics. The application of CDs in the food industry, including food packaging, is summarized in a concise manner. This paper sheds light on the current challenges and prospects of utilizing CDs in the packaging industry. It aims to provide researchers with a roadmap to tailor the properties of CDs to suit specific applications in the food industry, particularly in food packaging.

head.ce:title: Turning food waste into value-added carbon dots for sustainable food packaging application: A review

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a recent addition to the nanocarbon family, encompassing both crystalline and amorphous phases. They have sparked significant research interest due to their unique electrical and optical properties, remarkable biocompatibility, outstanding mechanical characteristics, customizable surface chemistry, and negligible cytotoxicity. Their current applications are mainly limited to flexible photonic and biomedical devices, but they have also garnered attention for their potential use in intelligent packaging. The conversion of food waste into CDs further contributes to the concept of the circular economy. It provides a comprehensive overview of emerging green technologies, energy-saving reactions, and cost-effective starting materials involved in the synthesis of CDs. It also highlights the unique properties of biomass-derived CDs, focusing on their structural performance, cellular toxicity, and functional characteristics. The application of CDs in the food industry, including food packaging, is summarized in a concise manner. This paper sheds light on the current challenges and prospects of utilizing CDs in the packaging industry. It aims to provide researchers with a roadmap to tailor the properties of CDs to suit specific applications in the food industry, particularly in food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a novel member of carbon-based nanomaterials. CDs are characterized by a quasi-spherical structure with a size of <10 nm comprising a nanocrystalline sp2-hybridized carbon core and an amorphous sp3-hybridized carbon shell [1,2]. The surface of CDs contains a variety of oxygenated functional groups, such as amino (−NH2), hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and carbonyl (-C=O) groups, contributing to remarkable chemical versatility, water solubility and functionalization [3,4]. CDs offer a unique array of exceptional features such as tunable optical properties (bright photoluminescence, strong absorption, superior resistance to photo-bleaching, and fluorescence stability), good biocompatibility, low-to-negligible cytotoxicity, affordable cost, excellent water solubility, ease of preparation, and large specific surface area which enable their applications in various engineering and biomedical fields (Fig. 1 ) [5].The attractive merits of CDs have sparked an immense interest across a wide range of applications, including the anti-counterfeiting [6], biomedicine [7], catalysis [8], sensing [9], photovoltaic devices [10], packaging [11], and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [12]. Besides, CDs have become increasingly popular as a truly sustainable filler in food packaging composites. Researchers have recently focused on using green natural precursors, which are renewable, affordable, and environment friendly, to make CDs with remarkable optical properties. Food processing by-products have immense potential to be converted into CDs. Using green chemistry principles, biomass-based CDs have been produced from pine pollen [13], saffron [14], papaya [15], silkworm chrysalis [16], Pseudostem of banana plant [17], holy basil [18], prawn shells [19], and lotus root [20]. Food waste is generated in abundance throughout the supply chain every day. The accumulation of food waste in the natural environment causes adverse economic and environmental impacts [21]. The European Union has estimated an annual production of 89 million tonnes of food waste, a number that is continuing to rise steadily [22]. In Australia, total food waste is estimated at around 2.5 million tonnes per year. Sustainable food waste management requires industries to transit towards a circular economy, converting waste into useful value-added products. Recent research has demonstrated the enormous potential of agro-industrial by-products, food, and kitchen wastes for bioconversion into worthy and valuable marketable products such as biochars [23], edible films [24], culture media [25], and nanoparticles [26]. The valorization of household food waste, such as peels of different fruits and vegetables, as carbon sources to generate CDs is one of the latest breakthroughs made in this field over the past few years. CDs are synthesized using various carbon- and nitrogen-containing precursors and different methods. Many studies have employed a wide range of carbon-rich precursors to generate CDs. For instance, nitrogen-doped CDs were produced via microwave radiation using acrylic acid and 1,2-ethylenediamine [27]. Additionally, a mixture of hydrazine hydrate, carbon nanopowder, polyurethane, and 1,2,4-triaminobenzene was used to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs [28–30]. Citric acid, a commonly available and typical carbon source, has been used in the synthesis of CDs through methods like hydrothermal and pyrolysis [31–33]. The use of natural resources and biomass waste introduces self-doping of desirable elements into CDs, as these resources are abundant in heteroatoms or functional groups. Notably, green synthesis of CDs often eliminates the need for additional surface modification. The promising application of biomass-derived CDs is demonstrated in various fields, including biomedical fields. The application of CDs as sensing fillers embedded within food packaging matrix appears to remain at an initial stage of development. Moreover, the synthesis of CDs with a minimal environmental impact and, at the same time, possessing excellent fluorescence and physicochemical properties still remains a challenge. Some review articles have been published with an emphasis on using various technologies to synthesize CDs from food waste [34,35]. However, applications of these CDs are often limited to biomedicine and energy storage/conversion, and the specific and multifaceted application of CDs in food-packaging sectors remains yet to be comprehensively reviewed. Hence, it is about time to undertake a systematic literature review and shed light on the prospects of CDs in packaging industries to spark new ideas towards real-life applications. This paper is divided into two sections. The first section provides an in-depth overview of different green methods used to synthesize CDs from a broad array of carbon sources, such as food waste, protein by-products, spent biomass, and polymeric waste, and makes comparisons in their physical, optical, antimicrobial, and structural properties. The second section focuses on reviewing fundamental knowledge and new developments on various functional properties of CDs relevant to their applications in food-packaging industries.

The rising concerns about global energy security and its impact on the environment have driven an interest in developing novel materials and processes [36]. Green synthesis of CDs refers to any production that seeks to harness environmentally friendly materials and/or energy-saving methods. The green process typically involves using natural precursors such as plant materials, microorganisms, food by-products, or other waste streams, aiming to avoid the use of toxic chemicals and/or complex and expensive equipment. With respect to the manufacturing of CDs, the goal of green synthesis is to reduce environmental implications and manufacturing costs while maintaining desirable quality and performance [37]. Using green technologies for the synthesis of CDs offers superior advantages compared to conventional synthetic approaches. These unique features not only reduce the creation of harmful waste but also conserve energy and minimize production costs. Additionally, repurposing discarded and unused waste materials to create value-added products is only feasible using certain methods. The customizable features, ample scalability, and cost-efficiency provide outstanding versatility and performance to these methods, which rival or even surpass traditional synthetic methods [38,39]. Although many research groups have made significant contributions towards establishing new procedures for the facile production of CDs, these methods often encounter problems that impede their large-scale adoption. These procedures often use tedious and energy-intensive processes that require the deployment of sophisticated instruments and chemicals and/or raw materials that are not environmentally benign. Continuous use of such unsustainable and costly processes not only increases the production costs and makes the finished products expensive but also does not embrace fundamental concepts of green chemistry [40,41]. To address environmental issues and high manufacturing costs of CDs, concepts of using environmentally benign and renewable materials to produce CDs have recently gained significant attention [42]. Although the total costs of CDs per gram have not been documented in the literature, CDs derived from cheap biomass precursors are expected to have significantly lower costs than those made with other precursors such as graphite, carbon fibers, and carbon nanotubes. A key question that needs to be answered is whether biomass-derived CDs have comparable photoluminescent properties to those derived from non-renewable materials. In addition to using green and inexpensive raw materials, green synthesis of CDs should use simple and straightforward technologies that consume less energy. The synthesis of CDs generally occurs through two pathways: top-down and bottom-up approaches [43]. The top-down approach refers to those methods that break down large carbon precursors into smaller (nanoscale) dimensions. There are three major top-down methods, namely laser ablation [44], arc discharge [45], and electrochemical oxidation [46]. It is difficult to implement key principles of green chemistry to a top-down approach because it requires specific equipment and high energy consumption. Hydrothermal synthesis or microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis are the most preferred methods over other synthetic approaches, such as laser ablation or arc-discharge synthesis, largely because of their greater alignment with the fundamental principles of green chemistry. The absence of sophisticated technological tools, potential to use natural biomass, and low operational reacting temperatures, which often fall within the range of 100-200 °C for the conversion of food waste materials into CDs, could establish these techniques as the method of choice compared to other techniques that require the operation of complex instrumentation using industrially impractical and uneconomical reacting temperatures above 500 °C. The laser ablation technique offers the unique advantage of enabling precise control over the characteristics of CDs. Adjusting various laser settings can ensure the high purity of CDs while allowing a wide variety of functionalization routes into their architecture. However, using this technique to convert biomass waste to CDs is hardly achievable due to the inherent drawbacks of the laser-ablation process, such as the requirement for advanced and costly laser systems, difficulty in fulfilling the process for natural waste biomass due to complex ablation mechanisms, inhomogeneous size, and lack of scale-up potential [47,48].

The arc discharge method for synthesizing CDs boasts the merits of simplicity, operational flexibility, and high energy efficiency to enable the synthesis of CDs with customizable properties. Nevertheless, its limitations encompass challenges in achieving precise control over size distribution and surface passivation due to the complex mechanisms that govern the formation of CDs. The precursor-dependent synthesis process requirement of certain equipment connected to a vacuum is another remaining obstacle linked to this technique. To qualify a technique for green synthesis, researchers should strike a balance between its advantages and drawbacks to amplify its effectiveness in crafting specialized CDs for diverse applications [49,50]. The electrochemical oxidation is another straightforward and environmentally friendly method for the synthesis of CDs. In this approach, controlled electrochemical oxidation of carbon-rich precursors within an electrolytic cell triggers the formation of CDs. The strengths of this technique include adaptability to diverse precursor sources, the capacity to manage characteristics through adjusting electrochemical factors, and the potential for scalability. Nonetheless, challenges remain surrounding energy consumption, production cost, and the difficulty of obtaining consistent structural properties. Based on these reasons, while this method has shown great potential for creating tailored CDs from synthetic precursors, its applicability to obtain CDs from different biomass sources on a large scale is still debated [48,51]. The bottom-up approach is based on the growth of nano-sized CDs from small carbon-containing molecules and polymers by stepwise chemical synthesis via hydrothermal [52], microwave [53], and ultrasound [54]. Given the flexibility, simplicity, and availability of vast raw materials, bottom-up approaches show better alignment with green chemistry principles. The procedures adopted in the bottom-up approach are more reliable and can be adapted in various fields because of their low cost, high yield, and environmentally friendly nature [55]. Despite the many benefits that the bottom-up approach offers, there are still many challenges to applying it for the conversion of low-value biomass to CDs. This is because the CDs obtained through the bottom-up approach can have low-crystallinity and non-uniform structures that make them unsuitable for certain applications. The hydrothermal carbonization method can immediately transform biomass into carbon-rich products, in contrast to other procedures that often need a pre-drying treatment of raw biomass [56]. Microwave-assisted carbonization also results in less waste and produces CDs with better consistency. Thus, it can shorten the time needed for the purification [57]. Currently, hydrothermal treatment is used as a popular platform for the green synthesis of CDs. In recent years, this method has been applied to many carbon-containing precursors such as vegetable and fruit wastes, protein by-products, microorganisms, and polymeric waste to produce CDs. CDs can be synthesized from plant parts (leaves, roots, fruits, vegetables, fruit peels, spices), animal parts, and other natural polymers utilizing bottom-up processes [58,59]. Another common method used to synthesize CDs is chemical oxidation, which uses a potent oxidizing agent [60]. A drawback associated with this method is the difficulty of removing excess oxidizing agents after synthesis [61]. To cope with this issue, several attempts have been made to create green procedures that can replace organic chemical precursors with biomass materials. The CDs synthesized using the green approach still require purification using post-treatment steps, which are often cumbersome and involve hazardous substances. A green synthesis method should be coupled with sustainable post-synthesis treatments to address environmental challenges [62]. A typical one-pot hydrothermal process used to produce CDs from fruit waste peels is illustrated in Fig. 2 . The properties of CDs are influenced by the synthesis routes used, which involve varying precursor materials, reaction conditions, and surface functionalization. The size and surface chemistry of the resulting CDs play a crucial role as they determine optical properties such as fluorescence emission, excitation wavelength, and quantum yield. Generally, smaller CDs exhibit blue-shifted emission and higher quantum yields. Moreover, the introduction or doping of heteroatoms through surface passivation/functionalization can modify their bandgap energy and emissive properties. Additionally, the surface passivation and functionalization of CDs have an impact on their colloidal stability, water dispersibility, and biocompatibility.

Liu et al. [54] employed a microwave-assisted synthesis technique to create hydrophilic CDs derived from denatured proteins. Bovine serum albumin, lipase, and pepsin were among the proteins denatured using urea or guanidine hydrochloride. To achieve denaturation, the protein (at a concentration of 9.0 × 10-5 mol/L) was mixed with a denaturing agent (at a concentration of 8.0 mol/L) and subjected to microwave irradiation at 700 W for 5 min. This process yielded a light-yellow product, which was subsequently purified through centrifugation and dialysis. The resulting CDs exhibited an average size ranging from 3 to 7 nm. The hydrophilic properties of these CDs were attributed to the presence of hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups on their surface. The CDs exhibited emission that depended on the excitation wavelength, with a peak emission at 400 nm when excited at 360 nm. The quantum yields of these CDs ranged from 8.6% to 14.4%, depending on the protein precursor used. The authors noted the potential applications of these protein-derived CDs as nanosensors for rapid detection of pH and temperature changes.

Atchudan et al. [55] synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs by subjecting Magnolia liliiflora flowers to a hydrothermal reaction at 240 °C for 12 h. The extracts from these flowers contained numerous nitrogen-rich compounds, making them ideal for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs. The resulting CDs were spherical and had an average size of around 4 ± 1 nm. Additionally, the quantum yield of these CDs was approximately 11%.

When compared to conventional hydrothermal synthesis of CDs that relies on conductive and convective heat transfer, microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis presents a distinct advantage of direct and precise heating of material internally by resonating the vibrational modes of molecules. This approach greatly enhances the conductive and convective heat transfer within the system, particularly in cases where the material being heated possesses high thermal conductivity. As a result, reaction times are significantly reduced, typically taking only a few minutes to complete. In contrast, traditional hydrothermal synthesis necessitates several hours to achieve a comparable outcome. However, microwave-based synthesis systems face significant constraints, such as localized overheating and challenges in scaling up the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs from by-products of plant origin
ce:section.ce:para: To deal with environmental problems associated with food waste, its valorization into valuable products is necessary [63]. CDs made from everyday food waste are often utilized because of self-doping heteroatoms such as nitrogen, phosphorus, or sulfur into CDs, which leads to higher quantum yields [64]. Plant wastes, which are produced in abundance across many food sectors, are very suitable starting materials for the synthesis of CDs because of their good biocompatibility, low cost, and non-toxicity [65]. Many studies examined using vegetal-based waste as a carbon source for producing CDs [66–70]. The synthesis of CDs from plant/vegetable by-products has great advantages of recovering and recycling waste by-products to produce high-value-added materials. In comparison with animal waste, vegetable waste often shows a higher yield of CD production. Based on this fact, a wide array of vegetal components such as fruit peels [60,71], fruit juice [72], and different parts of plants, including leaves [73], flowers [74–76], roots [27], seeds [77], and shells [78] have been utilized as a carbon precursor to making CDs. To efficiently convert these plant precursors into CDs, it is important to optimize the reaction temperature and time. A low reaction temperature may not efficiently carbonize precursors into CDs in a short time, whereas a shorter reaction time (at a high temperature) might not be sufficient to complete carbonization either. CDs with excellent water dispersibility, high photostability, and stability over an extensive pH range were obtained from citrus peels using a hydrothermal treatment carried out at 180 °C for 2 h. CDs with stable fluorescence and excellent stability in different pHs were also synthesized from microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization of onion peels [79]. Lie et al. [80] designed a method to make CDs from red radish by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 3 h. Since radish is rich in amino acids, carbohydrates, proteins, and vitamins, it is not surprising to obtain self-doped CDs with nitrogen and sulfur. CDs of various particle sizes and varying functional groups on their surface can be synthesized from a wide array of plants. An important benefit of using plant resources to make CDs is their simplicity of the process and low capital and operational cost [81]. Apart from vegetable by-products, cereal by-products, which are abundantly produced by food industries, are also appropriate natural resources for synthesizing CDs [82]. Coffee industry by-products have long been used as a valuable source of antioxidants and have a high potential for conversion into CDs. Zhang and his group used a moderate molecular aggregation technique to produce CDs from coffee bean husks by mixing them with an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. The precipitate was then washed with water, oven-dried, dialyzed, and then converted into CDs. The CDs obtained from coffee beans showed strong antioxidation properties due to naturally occurring phenolic compounds in them [83]. In another work, CDs were produced from walnut shells via a chemical breakdown [78]. Zhao and his colleagues obtained CDs from corn bract by treatment at 100 °C for 24 h under nitrogen flux, followed by further purification [66]. Table 1 summarizes recent studies that reported the synthesis of CDs from plant sources using different synthesis methods aimed at targeted applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs from the by-products of animal-origin
ce:section.ce:para: There are various methods that are adapted to synthesize CDs from sources of animal origin. Using animal sources to make CDs may not pose significant obstacles to commercializing these nanomaterials. For instance, collagen is a potent source of amino acids, provides a higher proportion of carbon content, and is considered an economical source for synthesizing CDs [108,109]. The use of animal-source by-products and their derivatives as potential natural precursors for synthesizing CDs has been widely studied (Table 2 ). The conversion of animal food waste or meat industry by-products is mostly performed via low-cost and eco-friendly hydrothermal techniques. Yuan et al. [110] obtained CDs by hydrothermally treating chicken blood. The authors mixed finely crushed chicken blood with distilled water before transferring it into a sealed autoclave and heated it at 180 °C for 10 h, followed by purification and dialysis steps. The resulting CDs had a broad size distribution (1.5 to 3.6 nm) and could emit bright blue fluorescence. Another promising benefit associated with using livestock wastes is producing CDs with better water solubility than plant-based resources [111]. The fact that seafood waste is already converted into chitin, chitosan, carotenoids, gelatins, and collagen makes it easier to obtain purer starting materials and provides many opportunities for synthesizing CDs [112]. Gedda et al. [19] used a green and economic strategy to synthesize CDs from prawn shells. These CDs had an average diameter of ∼4 nm and distinct fluorescence emission with a quantum yield of 9%. In addition, the authors demonstrated many other unique properties associated with prawn shell-derived CDs, including excellent homogeneity and monodispersity and good solubility in water. In a similar study, Jiang et al. [113] fabricated CDs from chitin using a hydrothermal treatment (240 °C for 10 h) followed by a centrifugation step (1000 rpm for 10 min) and a filtration step to remove undesired content. The synthesized CDs showed an average diameter of 4.21 nm and a strong photoluminescence quantum yield of 25.8% under UV light. These CDs also had excellent optical stability (light yellow under daylight and brilliant blue fluorescence under UV lamp) due to the abundance of nitrogen elements. Honey is a sugar-rich food containing many carbon atoms, and it can be carbonized to synthesize CDs. The CDs synthesized from honey via a one-step low-temperature heating method (100 °C for 2 h) had a high quantum yield (19.8%) and did not aggregate easily [114]. Bee pollen is another carbon source containing many compounds with diverse chemical structures (carbohydrates, proteins, amino acids, and nucleic acids [115]. CDs have been hydrothermally synthesized from bee pollen at 180 °C for 24 h. The CDs synthesized from bee pollen had an ultrasmall size (1–2 nm) and showed a remarkable biocompatibility [116]. Eggs may also be used to make nitrogen-doped CDs without passivation (the process of treating a material or surface to reduce its chemical reactivity) since they are considered a significant source of nitrogen with a wide range of different proteins [117]. Eggshell membranes and other egg by-products are a low-value waste of the egg processing industry, and they can be readily used as nitrogen and carbon precursors to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs. Pramanik et al. [118] synthesized CDs with adequate nitrogen co-doping from eggshells. CDs exhibited a negative surface charge value of –54.1 mV, a quantum yield of 8%, and a narrow particle size distribution (1–5 nm). In addition to eggshells, hydrothermal processing with microwave irradiation was used to produce CDs from the eggshell membrane. The CDs obtained this way showed a spherical shape with an average size of 3.75 nm [119]. Cow's milk is one of the healthiest foods and a rich source of carbon and nitrogen. Bajpai et al. [120] synthesized CDs from cow milk protein (beta-casein) through microwave treatment and achieved a quantum yield of 18.7%. Dairy waste streams containing lactose, whey protein, and casein have the potential to be carbonized into CDs.

The carbon of microbial origin obtained from biomass of various microorganisms provides plentiful opportunities for synthesizing CDs. This carbon source is gaining unprecedented attention as it enables the production of CDs in a sustainable manner. Microbial biomass is abundant in nature, can be obtained at a low cost, and can be readily processed [131]. The carbon source used to synthesize CDs is mainly obtained from microbial cells and/or their extracellular/intracellular metabolite products. Importantly, microbial cells are a rich source of organic compounds, including peptidoglycans, carbohydrates, peptides, and proteins containing heteroatoms such as sulfur, nitrogen, and phosphorus. Thus, the carbonization of this natural resource led to the synthesis of CDs with distinct structures and unique functional groups (amino groups and amide groups) without requiring additional passivation or modification [132,133]. Similar to other carbon sources, hydrothermal carbonization is a common method to synthesize CDs from the carbon of microbial biomass [134]. Hydrothermal synthesis of microbial biomass typically does not require temperatures higher than 250 °C. The cellular structure of microbial biomass is vulnerable within the temperature range commonly used for sterilization (100–121 °C). Applying a temperature of approximately 150 °C causes further structural damage and eliminates contamination. However, a relatively high temperature, such as 250 °C, is required in the hydrothermal carbonization process to effectively convert the remaining biomass into CDs. Carbonization conducted at temperatures below 250 °C leads to a reduced yield and lower quality CDs.

At temperatures above 250 °C, the thermal stability of the microbial cells is compromised, leading to the denaturation of cellular proteins and enzymes [135]. The carbonization of biomass can be achieved at relatively low temperatures, providing significant advantages in terms of reduced overall energy consumption and production costs [136]. The synthesis of CDs from microbial (bacteria, algae, and fungi) biomass has been studied in considerable detail (Table 3 ). For instance, CDs have been produced using biomass carbon from Lactobacillus acidophilus through a one-step hydrothermal process as a sustainable strategy to fabricate nano-papers with improved UV light barrier and antibacterial properties. The CD-embedded nano-papers displayed a homogeneous appearance with better stretchability and flexibility when compared with the untreated nano-papers. The outcome of this study indicated the promising nature of CDs obtained from lactic acid bacteria in increasing antimicrobial/antioxidant and UV protective properties of food packaging systems [134]. Plácido et al. [137] used biochar obtained from microalgae as the carbon source to synthesize CDs. The depolymerization reaction of biochar was carried out in an autoclave maintained at 120 °C and 103.421 kPa pressure for 1 h, followed by centrifugation and ultrasonication steps. The fluorescence properties of CDs obtained in this way were highly resistant to photo-bleaching and showed excellent stability across a broad range of pH. Microbial carbon can even be sourced from pathogenic microorganisms, which would require adequate health and safety protection. Qin et al. [133] synthesized CDs from Escherichia coli using a hydrothermal method and confirmed the capability of these CDs for bioimaging both dead and live bacterial cells. The authors reported that CDs obtained from the E. coli biomass were spherical in shape with a size of ∼2.9 nm. The XRD analysis showed a strong diffraction peak at 22°, which confirmed the graphitic structure of these CDs. In a similar work, Ghorbani et al. [131] developed CDs from Saccharomyces cerevisiae which had a high antioxidant capacity and excellent antimicrobial properties. The authors showed that these CDs had a dose-dependent antioxidant property and antimicrobial activity against some fungal and bacterial strains. A combined formulation of biomass-derived CDs and bacterial nanocellulose showed potent antibacterial activity against food-borne pathogenic bacteria. Furthermore, the antimicrobial/antioxidant properties of CDs synthesized from microbial biomass may provide unique opportunities for their usage as food additives.

CDs can be produced using many fungal strains through simple and scalable production methods. The process involves growing fungi and extracting materials required to produce CDs [131,138]. In addition to their ease of processing, fungi-derived CDs also exhibit excellent metal resistance, making them suitable for use in harsh environments. This property comes from carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the CDs produced fungal biomass that protects them from metal ions. Overall, the ability of fungi to biosynthesize CDs through their metabolic pathways, combined with their ease of processing, handling, and excellent metal resistance, make them a promising source of CDs suitable for various applications [139]. Fungal biomass usually contains a significant number of extracellular proteins, which promote the synthesis of CDs with higher stability and enhanced functionality [140]. Recent research has used several types of fungal biomass to produce CDs, including Ganoderma Lucidum [141], Ganoderma enigmaticum [142], Guignardia mangiferae [143], black fungus [144], and Aspergillus flavus [145]. Yu et al. [146] produced nitrogen-containing CDs of perhaps the smallest size (size of ∼2.9 nm) using carbon from Saccharomycetes and nitrogen from ethylenediamine through microwave-assisted heating. The authors then used these CDs to develop high-performance photoluminescence-based biosensor devices. These authors also demonstrated well-defined crystalline cores in fungal-derived CDs with desirable functional groups (carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, amino, and amide bonds). Such exceptional architecture of fungal-derived CDs can be preferably used as fluorescent nano-sensors. Yeast cells can also be used as a primary carbon source to produce CDs with exceptional optical characteristics. The feasibility of using microbial biomass from Cryptococcus podzolicus (C. podzolicus) to synthesize CDs through a hydrothermal treatment has been demonstrated by Ji et al. [147]. These CDs had quantum yields of 4% and zeta potential of −30.25 mV and were homogeneous in composition and spherical in shape with an average diameter of 4.8 nm. These CDs were colorless and transparent under visible light, while appearing blue under UV light excitation, indicating their fluorescence. Beer yeast has also been used to synthesize fluorescent CDs. These CDs had photoinduced bactericidal functions and unique fluorescence and photocatalytic properties. These outstanding characteristics, along with a quantum yield of ∼21% and a zeta potential of −41.7 mV for concentrations below 1 mg/mL, could be used as a dye material to measure bacterial viability [148].

Biobased polymer waste materials such as starch or cellulose have aroused considerable interest in synthesizing CDs. These two biopolymers are readily available in abundance from food-processing or paper-making industries [156]. Basu et al. [157] synthesized photoluminescent CDs with sizes of 4-5 nm from tapioca starch under mild conditions and used them to detect fluoride (F−) ions in water. Starch-derived CDs were synthesized by dissolving starch in hot alkaline water (80–90 °C) and agitating the solution for 12 h followed by neutralizing the solution using dilute hydrochloric acid. These CDs had spherical shape and sizes ranging from 4 to 5 nm. The surface of these CDs contained a significant number of carboxylic groups, which not only facilitated their dispersion in water but also aided in the attachment of metal cations. Another study synthesized fluorescent CDs from an aqueous starch solution by using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method [158]. The authors carefully monitored the formation of CDs to gain an understanding of the mechanisms of fluorescence and optical properties. These CDs emitted intense blue color at 435 nm and had a quantum yield of 30% upon UV photoexcitation. However, intense blue fluorescence was affected when samples were irradiated for an extended period of time (14 min). This indicated that microwave heating time should be carefully chosen to avoid creating nonfluorescent CDs. Researchers have also attempted to reduce the accumulation of polymeric waste materials (PLA, PET) in the environment by recycling them into CDs. Polymeric waste materials (starch) have been recycled to synthesize fluorescent CDs using solvothermal synthesis with HCl or NaOH [159], hydrothermal synthesis using polyethylene imine [160], pyrolysis of a mixture of water and polyethylene glycol [161], pyrolysis using sulfuric acid [162], microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis [163], and pyrolysis in water [164]. The packaging industry is currently generating an extraordinary quantity of polylactic acid (PLA) waste; therefore, there is a need for the cost-effective utilization of PLA waste. Lauria et al. [165] designed a green synthetic method to develop luminescent spherical (diameter of 3 nm) CDs by using PLA as a carbon-containing plastic precursor. PLA resins were hydrolytically degraded by soaking them in water at 95 °C for 3 weeks. The hydrolytic degradation broke down the PLA particles to <5 mm and increased the solubility in water. The hydrolytic scission of the PLA chain produced acidic products and lowered the pH of the medium to ∼2.3. The authors then synthesized CDs using a hydrothermal reaction of low-molecular-weight PLA oligomers at temperatures ranging from 180 to 240 °C for 120 h. The key advantages of this synthesis approach included simplicity of operation, catalyst-free conditions, and the ready availability of a starting carbon source. These features make the synthesis approach a potentially viable technique to reduce the impacts of plastic waste.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doping and surface functionalization of biomass-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Biomass-derived CDs have emerged as promising nanomaterials with unique functional properties and an environmentally friendly nature. To further enhance the functionality of biomass-derived CDs and tailor their properties for specific applications, many strategies have been explored to functionalize their surface properties. This section of the review briefly discusses two common approaches: heteroatom doping and surface functionalization.

Doping is a simple yet effective technique for modulating the optical, electronic, and chemical properties of CDs by introducing foreign atoms or molecules into their carbon backbone [166]. CDs with a wide range of functionalities can be produced by incorporating different heteroatom dopants during the synthesis process. Nitrogen-doping, in particular, has been extensively studied due to its ability to adjust the energy bandgap of CDs, resulting in improved optical properties like fluorescence quantum yield and photostability [167]. Other dopants such as sulfur, phosphorus, and boron have demonstrated their utility in modifying the electronic structure and optical properties of CDs [168,169]. Doping on CDS can be accomplished using two distinct methods: in situ synthesis and post-synthetic treatment [170,171]. These methods provide precise control over the concentration and distribution of dopants throughout the carbon structure, thereby impacting the ultimate properties of the CDs. Doped biomass-derived CDs have been applied in various fields, including bioimaging, sensing, photocatalysis, and energy storage, which highlights the significant potential of functionalization strategies. The surface functionalization involves the modification of the surface of CDs using specific functional groups or molecules to impart new properties or enable desired bioactivities to promote specific affinities to targeted substrates or biomolecules. This strategy provides a flexible platform for tailoring the surface chemistry, dispersibility, stability, and reactivity of CDs derived from biomass. Functional groups, such as amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, or thiol, can be introduced onto the surface of CDs using various methods, including covalent bonding and non-covalent interactions like electrostatic adsorption [172–174]. The introduction of these functional groups on the CDs allows for their conjugation with other biomolecules, polymers, or nanoparticles. This expands their range of applications in biomedicine, bioimaging, drug delivery, and biosensing. Moreover, surface functionalization of CDs is necessary to enhance their stability and dispersibility in different solvents or matrices, making it easier to include them in various composite materials and hydrogels.

In short, the functionalization of biomass-derived CDs through doping and surface modification techniques opens up new possibilities for tailoring their important properties and expanding their applications. By surface functionalizing biomass-derived CDs, we can pave the way for the development of highly functional and versatile devices with great potential for a wide range of everyday and high-tech applications.

Biomass-derived CDs have unique functional properties that make them attractive for various applications. These properties include biocompatibility, high photoluminescence, excellent stability, and tunable optical properties. Moreover, the functional groups present on their surfaces allow for functionalization, making them versatile for use in different fields. In this section, we will briefly discuss some of the functional properties of biomass-derived CDs.

Understanding the physical and structural properties of CDs is important to gain insights into the underlying mechanisms that govern their light-emitting and other characteristics. Different analytical methods have been used to investigate physical characteristics, crystal structure, and the number of reactive groups on the surface of CDs. The investigations on physical and structural characteristics show that the CDs derived from food waste and non-renewable carbon precursors reveal similar crystalline patterns and geometric shapes. For example, Rodriguez et al. [72] studied the structural and morphological properties of tomato juice-derived CDs. The evaluation of morphology and structure showed a graphite-like structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of ∼0.24 nm. These CDs had an average particle size of ∼9 nm. Given the variations in physicochemical attributes, it may not be possible to obtain generic or common spectroscopic features for different CDs. However, there are some spectral bands that are virtually identical in all types of CDs, irrespective of the carbon source used to produce them. CDs often show a broad band between 3020 cm−1 and 3600 cm−1 associated with stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups. A most interesting feature observed in FTIR spectra of CDs is an intense band appearing at 1030 cm-1. This band is attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C bonds. Carbonyl (C=O) groups of CDs also exhibit a strong absorbance band at 1604 cm-1. The spectral map of CDs also consists of two bands located at 1360 cm-1 and 1229 cm-1, representing vibrational modes of CO bonds. The presence of ester groups (-COO) in CDs can be indicated by the presence of three peaks appearing at 1100 cm-1, 1150 cm-1, and 1750 cm-1. Spherical and well-dispersed CDs with an average particle size of 5–8 nm were synthesized using starch as a carbon source [162]. The elemental composition of these CDs was found to be ∼1.1% nitrogen, 3.1% oxygen, and 95.8% carbon. A similar study synthesized cranberry bean-derived CDs through a hydrothermal treatment (200 °C for 18 h) without any post-modification treatment [175]. The authors found that CDs derived from cranberry beans showed uniform morphology, crystalline structure, spherical shape, and monodisperse particle size distribution (mean size ∼4 nm). They also tended not to aggregate. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging of these CDs clearly revealed the presence of (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon with a d-spacing value of 0.21 nm. Despite many focused research efforts to synthesize CDs from natural biobased sources, controlling their morphology size and shape still remains a major challenge.

The tunable properties of CDS are one of the reasons why they are attracting increased research interest. The tunable fluorescence properties of CDs facilitate their application in diverse fields, including sensing, biomedical imaging, drug delivery, optoelectronics, and packaging [176,177]. Fluorescence quantum yield, emission, and excitation wavelengths are three key parameters that govern the luminescence efficiency of CDs [178].

The fluorescence emission of biomass-derived CDs arises from multiple mechanisms, including size-dependent quantum confinement and surface functionalization. Their suitability for food packaging applications can be greatly increased by optimizing these mechanisms. Similar to conventional CDs, biomass-derived CDs also display size-dependent optical properties as a result of quantum confinement effects [179]. The size of CDs can be altered to yield specific fluorescence emission by adjusting key synthesis parameters, including precursor concentration and reaction condition. Smaller CDs generally display higher fluorescence intensity and emit light at shorter wavelengths [180]. Biomass-derived CDs can be surface-functionalized with various molecules or polymers if changes in their fluorescence properties are required [181]. Surface functionalization can enhance the stability, photoluminescence efficiency, and selectivity of CDs intended for specific analytes or food packaging. By carefully selecting the biomass and synthesis methods and applying surface functionalization, the fluorescence properties of biomass-derived CDs can be harnessed to improve quality control, tracking, and monitoring pathogens in the food packaging [182,183]. The strong affinity of CDs towards metal cations, along with their tunable fluorescence properties, means that they can be preferably applied to detect toxic heavy metal ions in wastewater [184]. Several attempts have been made to demonstrate the fluorescence quenching effects of various metal ions on CDs. The fluorescence properties of CDs synthesized from natural biobased sources are listed in Table 4 . For instance, Kumar et al. [25] developed a facile and rapid method to synthesize fluorescent CDs from leaves of holy basil (Ocimum sanctum) by using a hydrothermal method. These CDs were stable in water for a long time (months) and showed strong fluorescence emission (quantum yield ∼9.3%). The authors then used metal ion-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs to explore their potential applications as fluorescent sensors for detecting Pb2+ ions. The quenching efficacy of these CDs was studied over a wide range of pH values (2.0 to 12.0). When the pH was varied from 2.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence intensity of CDs significantly increased and then tended to decrease when the pH was increased from 7.0 to 12.0. The highest fluorescence intensity with a better sensitivity towards Pb2+ ions was observed at pH 7.0. The highly sensitive performance of these CDs towards Pb2+ ions was indicated by the detection limit of 0.59 μM. A critical question that needs to be answered is whether metal ion-induced fluorescence quenching occurs only in water or also in other solvents. To answer this question, Li et al. [185] synthesized CDs with prominent fluorescence properties using a vegetable (Hongcaitai) via a hydrothermal method (240 °C for 20 h). The authors observed that too low temperature or too short reaction time caused insufficient carbonization of the precursors, which ultimately led to a low quantum yield. On the contrary, too high temperature or too long reaction time resulted in a decrease of surface functional groups and led to a similarly low quantum yield. Interestingly, CDs were only marginally soluble in ethanol despite having extraordinarily high solubility in water. The ethanol-soluble CDs had a quantum yield of 17.8%, whereas the water-soluble CDs had a quantum yield of 21%. Both CDs showed notable fluorescence quenching ability in the presence of hypochlorite (ClO−) and mercuric (Hg2+) ions. The water-soluble CDs showed higher sensitivity towards Hg2+ ions with a detection limit of 0.06 μM. On the other hand, the ethanol-soluble fluorescent CDs had a greater sensitivity towards ClO− as indicated by the detection limit of 0.015 μM. These findings demonstrate the importance of the solubility and stability of CDs in imparting desirable fluorescence quenching effects [185]. CDs were synthesized by Thambiraj and Shankaran [105] from sugarcane waste using toluene-induced chemical oxidation. These sugarcane-derived CDs had high stability and a large quantum yield (18.7%). The CDs extracted with toluene had distinct multicolor fluorescence emission peaks at 327, 338, 408, and 438 nm upon excitation at 283 nm. The quantum effects, surface area, and varying emissive traps on the surface of CDs have been identified as key parameters that determine the strength of fluorescence properties. The application of fluorescent CDs for bioimaging or as a biosensor is widely investigated. However, it remains relatively unexplored whether CDs can be used to detect metabolites associated with food spoilage. There is ongoing research that uses the sensing ability of CDs to detect toxic fungal or bacterial metabolites produced during food spoilage. The sensitivity of CDs with pH is another important driver that makes them beneficial for detecting food spoilage. Indeed, deterioration in food can be detected by a change in the color of CDs or a change in their fluorescence intensity after exposure to different pH values. When many foods start to spoil, their pH drops to acidic levels as a result of bacterial or fungal activities that release acidic compounds. This decrease in pH can cause a change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs, ultimately offering a fast and non-invasive method for detecting food spoilage. Kilic et al. [186] developed colorimetric fish gelatin films containing red cabbage anthocyanin extract to monitor the freshness of food products. Anthocyanins-doped gelatin films were further treated with CDs and UV irradiation as chemical and physical crosslinkers, respectively. The kinetic colorimetric response of CDs containing films was investigated against volatile ammonia generated by simulated food spoilage. UV-treated gelatin films containing 100 mg/L CDs showed higher sensitivity to ammonia gas. When the pH of the gelatin film solution changed from neutral to acidic, CDs showed luminescence with a bright yellow color, whereas, when the pH gradually shifted to the basic/alkaline range, the color of the CDs changed from bright yellow to yellowish orange. Similar pH-dependence of CDs' color was reported before and was linked to electronic transition changes between π − π* and η − π* in nanodomains by refilling or depleting their valence bands [84]. The authors confirmed a similar pattern of change of color in colorimetric films when they placed chicken breast samples [186].

The fluorescence properties of CDs are highly sensitive to variations in pH due to the ionization of surface functional groups [190]. This feature can make them useful for many food applications. Food poisoning and/or spoilage is one of the key challenges throughout the supply chain, which can be determined by monitoring pH variations. The pH-responsive emission of CDs can rapidly respond to pH variation, which can then be used to monitor spoilage [186]. Fluorescence emission is generated when a substance absorbs light or other electromagnetic radiation. Therefore, the pH-responsiveness of CDs could be explained by reversible molecular change within their structure, which influences their electron distribution and fluorescence intensity. The pH-sensitive emission of CDs arises from changes in the relative position or orientation of two fluorophores, which function as a donor-acceptor pair. It is also considered that the pH-responsive behavior of CDs originates from pH-induced deprotonation of carboxyl groups, which could induce electrostatic charge in them [191]. However, this postulation relies on the properties of bulk organic materials and may not explain the pH-dependence fluorescence of zero-dimensional systems such as CDs [192]. Despite several efforts to understand the underlying mechanisms of the pH responsiveness of CDs, there is no consensus on these mechanisms. One of the propositions used to explain the different fluorescence intensities of CDs at different pH environments is protonation theory. This theory explains that deprotonation and protonation alter the proton transfer energy levels. Omidi et al. [193] investigated changes in the emission intensity of CDs excited at 365 nm under different pH environments. To gain insights into pH-induced alteration on the structure of CDs, these authors determined the florescence of a fixed concentration of CDs (in water) in varying pHs (2.0-12.0). The visual appearance of the solution of CD at different pH values was determined under visible daylight and UV illumination (Fig. 3a). The color of CD solutions kept under daylight changed from bright-yellow to dark-yellow with increasing pH. The pH-dependence of CDs could be more clearly detected under UV light. The fluorescence intensity of CDs shows a clear increase in its intensity when the pH is changed from acidic to alkaline (Fig. 3a). The high sensitivity of CDs to lower pH values could be explained by deprotonation of carboxylic acid groups on their surface that gives rise to aggregation-induced quenching. The results from this study showed the applicability and high potential of CDs to use as a pH-sensitive indicator. Another research [194] further confirmed the above finding by systematically investigating the effects of surface modifications on pH-sensitivity and optical properties of CDs. The solubility of CDs in water was increased when their surface was modified with metal ions, alkalis, or acids, as compared to the unmodified ones. The authors observed that CDs that had Cu2+ on their surface were more pH-sensitive and exhibited distinct emission colors. The CDs that contain metal ions are particularly suitable for pH-based colorimetric measurements. (Fig. 3b). Therefore, surface modification is an effective approach to improve the pH-sensitivity of CDs as it positively alters absorption characteristics, fluorescence emission, and dispersibility in various solvents. Das et al. [195] studied the emission stability of jute-derived fluorescent CDs in alkaline and acidic environments by monitoring fluorescent signals at different pH values. They reported a significant increase in the fluorescence intensity of CDs when pH was changed from 1 to 7; however, a further increase in pH values decreased the intensity (Fig. 3b). The authors attributed this phenomenon to the variation of protonation degrees of CDs as a function of pH. The protonation of surface functional groups was likely responsible for the weak fluorescence signals of CDs in the acidic pH range. The fluorescence intensity of CDs rapidly increased to neutral (7.0) pH. The fluorescence signals of CDs were easier to quench in alkaline conditions due to the formation of aggregation (Fig. 3c). The fluorescent signals of CDs were also studied in ionic (NaCl) conditions and found that the intensity remained stable up to 1.0 M/mol concentration (Fig. 3c). This indicates that CDs can be used in solutions with varying ionic strength.

CDs are shown to impart antimicrobial properties; however, the exact mechanism through which they impart this effect is not yet adequately elucidated [196,197]. Their extremely small size could be the factor imparting the antimicrobial functionality. The small particle size of CDs facilitates their penetration through bacterial cell membranes. The presence of CDs within the microbial cells interferes with their metabolic function. Food materials and products lose their quality (e.g., color, taste, texture, and smell) due to microorganism-induced spoilage [198]. Therefore, CDs with antimicrobial activities can offer additional opportunities for applications in food packaging. One of the notable functional properties of biomass-derived CDs is their inherent antimicrobial activity and remarkable capacity to be combined with other antimicrobial agents. CDs have antimicrobial properties that make them promising candidates for food packaging applications where preventing microbial growth of packaged foods is important [199]. There are several mechanisms that can explain the antimicrobial activity of biomass-derived CDs. The small size and large surface area of these CDs facilitate enhanced contact with microorganisms, resulting in more effective killing effects. CDs can physically interact with microbial cell membranes, causing disruption and leakage of intracellular components. This ultimately leads to cell death and inhibition of microbial growth [200]. The generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is another proposed mechanism through which biomass-derived CDs exhibit antimicrobial properties. Upon exposure to light or other external stimuli, CDs can produce ROS, such as singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals. These accumulated ROS exert antimicrobial effects by inducing oxidative damage to microbial cells and impairing their viability [201].

When incorporating biomass-derived CDs into food packaging materials, it is important to carefully consider design principles to preserve their antimicrobial properties. Several factors need to be considered to achieve the desired antimicrobial efficacy. The choice of biomass sources used to obtain the CDs is of primary importance, as different types of biomasses can yield CDs with varying levels of antimicrobial activity. CDs synthesized from biomass that naturally contains antimicrobial compounds, such as citrus peels or tea leaves, may exhibit enhanced antimicrobial activity [202,203]. The choice of synthesis method used can influence the structure, surface charge, size, surface chemistry, and functional properties of CDs [204]. The bactericidal activity of biomass-derived CDs can be enhanced by post-synthesis functionalization with other bioactive ingredients or functional polymers [205]. Functionalization of CDs can be achieved using various surface modification techniques, regardless of the carbon source employed. These techniques include those based on covalent bonding or electrostatic interactions [206,207]. The controlled release of antimicrobial agents from biomass-derived CDs offers the flexibility to achieve sustained release over an extended period. This can be accomplished by either encapsulating the bioactive agents within the internal structure of the CDs or anchoring them to the outer surface [208]. Such systems can have great potential to enhance the shelf life of packaged foods [209]. Remarkable progress has been made regarding the incorporation of bactericidal CDs into edible coatings or films and their application to extend the shelf-life of food products. The antimicrobial potency of white mulberry-derived CDs was examined against L. monocytogenes (Fig. 4a). Notable antimicrobial activities of these CDs against L. monocytogenes were observed, as indicated by a large inhibition zone. The antimicrobial activity of CDs may stem from the difference in zeta potentials between microorganisms and CDs, which increases binding potential. The nature and degree of surface charge of CDs and the target microbial cells can affect how well the CDs interact with the microorganisms. For example, Salimi et al. [210] reported that both L. monocytogenes and CDs had negatively charged surfaces with zeta potential values of −2.5 mV and − 44.5 mV, respectively. Since both CDs and bacteria had negative surface charges, the interaction between these two surfaces was unlikely due to electrostatic repulsion. Kousheh et al. [134] synthesized CDs from lactic acid bacteria through a hydrothermal method (200 °C for 24 h) and examined their antibacterial activities against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. The authors reported that these CDs had pronounced antimicrobial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli at 500 mg/mL concentration, as indicated by ∼20.16 mm and ∼ 15.38 mm inhibition zones, respectively (Fig. 4b). The bacterial cell wall is a critical factor that determines their protection against CDs. Gram-positive bacteria such as L. monocytogenes are characterized by a thick cell wall made of peptidoglycan, while Gram-negative bacteria like E. coli are surrounded by a relatively thin cell wall containing a peptidoglycan layer and an additional outer membrane made of lipids [211]. The bacterial cell wall can have a decisive role in resisting the penetration of CDs into the interior of cells. However, it depends on the surface charge and chemistry of the cell wall and the CDs. Indeed, positively charged CDs are more likely to absorb onto negatively charged bacterial cell walls via electrostatic interactions and deliver better antimicrobial activity. Due to the presence of multiple negatively charged functional groups (e.g., carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl) on the surface of CDs, the zeta potential of CDs is often negative, and such CDs would be less effective against bacterial strains such as E. coli and L. monocytogenes which have negatively charged surface. It is also suggested that bacterial strains with low surface charge (low zeta potential), e.g., L. monocytogenes, are also susceptible to CDs-induced inactivation and death as they cannot resist interaction with and then inward penetration of CDs [134]. Another study by a research team led by Fan et al. [212] developed a coating composed of chitosan and CDs and investigated its ability to prolong the shelf life of fresh-cut cucumbers. The authors studied the antibacterial activity of CD/chitosan coatings against S. aureus and E. coli to prove their effectiveness in preserving fresh-cut fruits. The authors reported that this coating was able to effectively inhibit/kill E. coli and S. aureus at 1.5% to 4.5% concentration of CDs, respectively. The higher inhibitory activity was observed at higher concentrations of CDs in the coating. The authors further showed that the formulations containing CDs and chitosan had potent inhibitory activity regardless of the CDs' concentration. This provided additional confirmation of greater susceptibility of Gram-positive bacteria to CDs than Gram-negative bacteria. In addition to antibacterial activity, the authors demonstrated that the CD/chitosan coatings had strong antifungal activity [212]. Fu et al. [183] assessed synergistic antibacterial effects between chitosan, gelatin, and CDs against S. aureus and E. coli and reported that chitosan/gelatin/CD films showed stronger bactericidal effects than chitosan/gelatin films against these two bacterial strains (Fig. 4c). Thus, incorporation of bio-based packaging with CDs could be a promising strategy to preserve food products which are susceptible to microorganism-induced spoilage. Similarly, Mei et al. [213] studied the antibacterial activity of silk sericin/chitosan films containing CDs against S. aureus and E. coli to gain insight into their synergistic effects and showed that the films containing CDs had stronger antimicrobial activity against both S. aureus and E. coli (Fig. 4d). The stronger antimicrobial activity of silk sericin/chitosan films containing CDs could be due to bactericidal topography created by the presence of CDs. Such topographies could pierce through and rupture bacterial cells, which results into the leakage of intracellular constituents and the death of bacteria. The outcome of this study suggested that proper use of CDs in food packaging systems could provide new opportunities for preserving fruits and minimizing their losses during distribution and transport. Das et al. [195] studied the antibacterial activity of jute-derived CDs modified with benzalkonium chloride against S. aureus and E. coli. Their strong growth inhibition demonstrated the substantial bactericidal effect of these modified CDs against both bacterial strains. The minimum inhibitory concentrations of these modified CDs for S. aureus and E. coli were determined to be 0.48 and 0.03 μg/mL, respectively. These findings indicated that those CDs could be modified to enhance their antimicrobial activity against broad bacterial strains. The authors [195] then sought to link the bactericidal mechanisms of CDs with metabolic processes. They reported that binding of CDs to bacterial cell membranes promoted the generation of ROS, particularly hydroxyl radicals. These ROS impaired cell functions caused destruction to the integrity of cells and ultimately killed the bacteria (Fig. 5 ).

Antioxidant activity is another important functional property of CDs that can open new opportunities for their potential application in food packaging systems [214–216]. Antioxidants play an important role in preventing or reducing oxidative damage induced by oxygen and ROS in food products. Incorporating biomass-derived CDs with antioxidant properties into food packaging materials can mitigate oxidative deterioration and preserve the quality of packaged foods for longer [217]. Two possible mechanisms elucidate the antioxidant capacity of biomass-derived CDs. First, CDs possess a high density of functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, on their surface. These functional groups impart antioxidant and free radical scavenging abilities; thus, they are able to neutralize harmful ROS by donating electrons or hydrogen atoms (Fig. 6 ) [218]. Second, biomass-derived CDs can act as electron reservoirs or electron transporters, which enables them to effectively quench ROS and prevent their propagation in food systems. CDs can interrupt the free-radical chain reaction through electron transfer or proton-coupled electron transfer, which enables them to prevent the oxidation of oxygen-sensitive foods and preserve their quality [218,219]. To make the best use of the antioxidant properties of biomass-derived CDs in food packaging applications, it is important to select those with high antioxidant capacity. This selection process itself depends on factors such as the nature of the biomass, synthesis method, and surface functionalization of the CDs [220,221]. Many studies have shown the feasibility of using CDs for food packaging applications due to their strong antioxidant and radical scavenging activities. The composite films containing serine and CDs showed substantial antioxidant and free radical-scavenging capacities. Films containing CDs showed superior radical scavenging activity towards 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) free radicals [213]. CDs obtained from potato peels through a hydrothermal method (200 °C for 6 h) revealed their concentration-dependent antioxidant activity when assessed using DPPH and ABTS methods. The high free radical scavenging capacity of CDs could be related to the presence of numerous electron-donating groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface, which participate in reducing and quenching ABTS and DPPH radicals to generate more stable molecules. The free radical scavenging ability of gelatin/CD films was evaluated, and it was found that the addition of 4% CDs into gelatin films increased the ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging activities to 99.6% and 93.6%, respectively. The high antioxidant activity of gelatin films that contained CDs was attributed to amine or carboxylic groups of CDs that could facilitate the free radicals scavenging ability [222]. Rodríguez-Varillas et al. [72] carried out a comparative study to determine whether tomato-derived CDs had higher antioxidant activities than glutathione-derived CDs. The DPPH scavenging capacity was reported to be 54.7% to 63.8% for these CDs at concentrations of 4 and 20 ppm, respectively. The findings outline the importance of natural antioxidants such as CDs in conferring significant inhibitory activity against DPPH radicals. Interestingly, these tomato-derived CDs required less concentration than glutathione-derived CDs to achieve the same level of antioxidant activity. This level of effectiveness of tomato-derived CDs can be attributed to the presence of other natural antioxidants that augment the antioxidant property. Particle size, shape, and surface characteristics can also be the additional factors that contribute to the higher antioxidant activity of tomato-derived CDs as they facilitate greater interactions with free radicals [72]. Koshy et al. [223] developed an intelligent biopolymer film comprised of potato starch, anthocyanins from butterfly pea (Clitoria ternatea) flowers, and soy protein isolate-derived CDs. The DPPH radical scavenging capacity of these films increased by a ten-fold due to the synergistic effect of CDs and anthocyanins. Roy and colleagues synthesized CDs from mushrooms using a hydrothermal method (200 °C for 6 h) and incorporated them into gelatin/carrageenan films. The free radical scavenging activities of these CDs alone and composite films containing these CDs were determined. The antioxidant activity of these CDs itself was ∼50.3% at 75 μg/mL and ∼ 90% at 200 μg/mL in terms of DPPH assay (Fig. 7a and b). The superior antioxidant capacities of these CDs could be due to rapid ionization and subsequent hydrogen transfer from their hydroxyl groups and DPPH radicals. The authors also reported that the addition of 1 wt% CDs to carrageenan/gelatin film increased the free radical scavenging activity of the resulting film from 7 to 65% in the ABTS method and DPPH method (Fig. 7c and d). At a higher loading of CDs (5 wt%) into the film solution, ABTS and DPPH scavenging activities achieved their maximum values of ∼95% and ∼ 30%, respectively [224]. Incorporating a small amount of banana-derived CDs (∼0.25%) into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films had a remarkable impact on their ability to scavenge 31% DDPH and 84.5% ABTS free radicals (Fig. 7c and d). This outcome is particularly noteworthy as films produced using neat PVA do not have radical scavenging activity. ABTS radical scavenging activity of PVA/CD films increased and reached a maximum value (97.8%) when the concentration of CDs was 0.5%. No further increase of ABTS radical scavenging activity was observed in these films above 0.5% concentration of CDs. The ABTS scavenging capacity of these PVA/CD films was higher than the DPPH scavenging capacity, most probably due to faster hydrolytic degradation of the PVA matrix in ABTS aqueous solution than in DPPH methanolic solution, which promoted the faster release of CDs [85].

The antioxidant activity of starch/κ-carrageenan films containing clove-derived CDs was studied by Das et al. [86]. The authors reported a dose-dependent antioxidant behavior of CDs up to 650 μg/mL concentration. The addition of 4 mg CDs into the starch/κ-carrageenan film matrix achieved 20% DPPH and 66% ABTS scavenging activities. When this film matrix was loaded with 20 mg CDs an impressive 92% ABTS and 90% DPPH radicals scavenging capacities were achieved (Fig. 7e and f).

Excessive ultraviolet (UV) irradiation can pose serious health risks, including damage to the skin and immune systems [225,226]. Exposure to UV light also causes damage to materials due to their relatively high energy levels. UV irradiation occurs in the spectral range between 210 and 400 nm. Different types of UV filters are applied to polymeric materials to promote UV-absorbing properties. These UV-absorbing polymers are then used to protect food products from UV irradiation [226]. A notable problem that hinders the application of commonly available UV filters in the food packaging industry is their poor transparency. To overcome this problem, the application of UV filters obtained from organic substances has been proposed.

Biomass-derived CDs possess a unique ability to block harmful UV radiation, which is known to cause photochemical reactions leading to the degradation of food products and the loss of their quality and nutritional value. Incorporation of biomass-derived CDs with UV-blocking properties into food packaging materials can effectively safeguard packaged foods from UV-induced damage [189]. These CDs possess a distinctive atomic structure that enables them to absorb and dissipate UV emission effectively, preventing it from reaching the packaged foods [227]. The size and surface charge of CDs can influence their UV-blocking properties, making it possible to enhance their UV absorption by controlling their particle size and surface chemistry [228]. CDs possessing light-harvesting ability have recently emerged as sustainable absorbers of UV light. Thus, several attempts have been made to investigate the UV absorption mechanisms of CDs. Chen et al. [229] produced a UV absorber by mixing polyurethanes and CDs synthesized through a hydrothermal treatment (200 °C for 8 h). The inclusion of 5 wt% CDs in polyurethane films showed nearly 100% absorption of light under the UV range. Interestingly, these films retained high transmittance (∼ 90%) in the visible spectral regions. The authors also highlighted the long-term stability of CD-embedded polyurethane films when exposed to UV irradiation for 288 h. The UV shielding efficacy of these films decreased only marginally (by 2%) in 288 h of exposure. Chen et al. [229] explored the application of CD-embedded polyurethane films on light-emitting diode (LED) lamps to prevent leakage of UV. When the LED lamps were coated with CDs containing polyurethane, a significant reduction in UV emission was observed. Min et al. [222] observed that the color of CDs solution changed from brown to intense green after excitation with a UV light largely due to the quantum confinement effect. The CD's visible spectrum displays only one absorption peak at 310 nm due to the n-π* transition of either COH or CO bonds. Indeed, nano-sized CD particles could block the transmission of most short-wavelength UV light while enabling the transmission of visible light. The UV absorption capability of CDs is responsible for their UV-blocking feature. Campalani et al. [96] synthesized CDs from fish scales through hydrothermal treatment (200 °C for 24 h). Films were prepared by mixing gelatin with different percentages of CDs (1-5%). The gelatin films loaded with 5% CDs were able to block almost 70% of UV light. These films maintained 84.4% visible light transmittance, which was close to the 88.6% transmittance of neat gelatin films. The aggregation of CDs in the gelatin film matrix was considered to be the probable reason for this small decrease in transparency. These outcomes clearly indicated that the UV-blocking properties of CDs could substantially increase the UV shielding properties of packaging films. Such packaging films can be used to block/minimize UV light exposure and consequently extend their shelf life. UV barrier properties and transparency of CD-embedded packaging material were studied using gelatin/carrageenan films as the model [224]. The light transmittance of these films was measured at visible (660 nm) and UV (280 nm) regions. The incorporation of CDs in the gelatin/carrageenan matrix did not affect the transmittance of visible light, while the transmittance in UV light was substantially restricted. This observation further confirms the ability of CDs to prevent the transmission of UV light. This research also demonstrated that CDs with smaller particle size are more effective in blocking the transmission of short-wavelength UV light without affecting the transmission of visible light.

Water vapor and oxygen barrier properties play a crucial role in defining the quality and safety of food packaging materials. A package with a sufficient barrier to water and oxygen not only prevents moisture-related spoilage and maintains texture but also inhibits the entry of oxygen to preserve taste and nutritional value. The lifespan of a food package can be significantly extended by creating a protective shield against the infiltration of water vapor and oxygen [230]. CDs have emerged as promising nanofillers with the potential to enhance the water vapor and oxygen barrier performance of food packaging materials. The unique structural characteristics of CDs can be seamlessly leveraged to tailor the barrier properties of packaging materials, acting as effective barriers to impede the transmission of water and oxygen molecules. The high reinforcing ability of CDs that imparts exceptional barrier performance presents numerous opportunities to mitigate moisture-induced spoilage, ultimately leading to the extension of the shelf life of packaged products.

Campalani et al. [96] indicated that incorporating CDs into the gelatin films reduced the water barrier properties of the composite films by up to 25%. This observation was elucidated by considering the fact that CDs made a tortuous and impermeable path, reducing the diffusion rate of water molecules across the films. The tortuosity of the path for the water molecules and the capacity of CDs to establish cross-linking with gelatin reduced the free volume space within the polymeric matrix, yielding lower WVP. Notably, CDs can create an extended and convoluted route for water vapor transport in thin films, constituting a fundamental factor in the decrease of WVP. Kilic et al. [186] reported that the WVP of the films exhibited a substantial decrease (58.18%) when the concentration of CDs increased within the gelatin matrix. Significant levels of crosslinking are necessary to achieve a pronounced reduction in the WVP of highly hydrophilic protein-based films. Thus, this reduction could be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs and the gelatin matrix, which could have decreased the availability of hydrophilic sites for the attachment and subsequent permeation of water vapor. In another study, incorporating CDs diminished the cellulose nanofiber-based composite films' OTR from 5.08 cc/m2 day to 0.01 cc/m2 day. Enhanced oxygen barrier characteristics of the composite film might arise from the formation of synergistic interfacial bonding between CDs and polymers, yielding a denser structure with less free volume [182]. Wen et al. [231] showed that the chitosan nanocomposite films reinforced with CDs could effectively minimize the WVP and OTR. It is evident that augmented linkages with CDs could be a key reason for a significant reduction in the WVP value. Under a similar concept, the composite CD-containing chitosan film demonstrated remarkable OTR characteristics, displaying a downward trend with the increasing CD content from 0.25 to 1 wt%. A more compact and dense structure to the film could account for the outstanding improvements in oxygen barrier properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of packaging materials imparted by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties of polymeric films define their applications in food and non-food packaging. Thinner yet stronger packaging films are preferable as they require fewer materials [232]. The outstanding ability of CDs to alter and improve the mechanical properties of packaging materials means that they have great potential to be used in the food packaging sector. Packaging materials need to possess adequate strength, flexibility, and durability to protect and preserve the integrity of packaged foods. Incorporating biomass-derived CDs into packaging materials can improve their mechanical performance [224]. The improvement of mechanical properties in packaging materials upon incorporation of biomass-derived CDs can be attributed to their reinforcing effect.
