Biomass-derived CDs can act as a nanoscale reinforcing agent in packaging materials as they enhance tensile strength, tensile modulus, and impact resistance. The uniform dispersion of CDs within the packaging matrix and the formation of a reinforcing network hinder the crack propagation and improve the overall mechanical stability [85]. Moreover, biomass-derived CDs can improve the overall mechanical performance of the packaging material by promoting interfacial compatibility and better adhesion among different components present in packaging materials. [233]. The particle size and loading levels of CDs determine the extent to which they can improve their mechanical properties. Therefore, the production of CDs with uniform particle size and their uniform incorporation is important for achieving the required reinforcement effect without compromising the flexibility and processability of the packaging materials [234]. Surface functionalization of biomass-derived CDs can further improve their compatibility with packaging materials and enhance interfacial adhesion. An adequate surface modification or modification of the surface chemistry of CDs can enhance their interaction with the matrix material, which will ultimately improve the mechanical properties [235]. Thus, the incorporation of CDs in food packaging can enhance the mechanical integrity of packaging and protect foods from external damage. Studies have identified that there is a substantial potential for CDs to improve the mechanical properties of food packaging materials. Mei et al. [213] studied the mechanical properties of silk sericin/chitosan films reinforced with CDs. Doping 20% of CDs into the film matrix increased elongation at break and decreased the tensile strength of the CD-containing films. The significant increase in elongation at break was attributed to the high propensity of CDs to form hydrogen bonds with glycerol and sericin, thus increasing space between molecules and enabling higher polymer segment mobility. The reason behind the reduced tensile strength upon the addition of CDs could be the increased moisture content of films brought about by their hydrophilic characteristics. Konwar et al. [236] synthesized CDs from tea and used them to reinforce chitosan films. When the chitosan matrix was reinforced with 0.5 wt%, the resulting films achieved a 3.73-fold increase in tensile strength compared to neat chitosan films. The elongation at break of these re-enforced films decreased by 20% due to the stiffening of the matrix. The reduction in elongation at break was attributed to the cross-linking effect between CDs and the chitosan matrix, which restricted the mobility of chitosan. The superior mechanical strength of CD-enforced chitosan films might have been caused by the strong electrostatic attraction between negatively charged CDs and positively charged chitosan. However, the authors noted that a further increase in CDs concentration reduced tensile strength, which was attributed to the aggregation of CDs in a chitosan matrix and the resulting weak interaction between CDs and the chitosan matrix. In another work, Salimi et al. [210] produced a type of paper combining bacterial nanocellulose and CDs. It was observed that the incorporation of CDs at a concentration of 530 g/L improved the strain to break by 19%; however, the tensile strength decreased substantially. The higher stretchability of CD-embedded paper could be attributed to the hydrophilic character of CDs. The reduction of the tensile strength of the paper could also be related to weak interfacial strength between CDs and bacterial nanocellulose.

Understanding the cytotoxicity of CDs is necessary to ensure that they can be used in food packaging. Given the direct relationship between packaging films, food products, and migration of additives, materials, and fillers intended to be used in food packaging must be non-toxic. Despite the huge interest in using CDs in food packaging industries, there is a lack of convincing information regarding the potential cytotoxic effects of CDs on humans. A limited number of (in vitro and in vivo) studies attempted to determine the cytotoxicity of CDs by themselves or after embedding them into films. Ezati et al. [182] studied the cytotoxicity of cellulosic films containing nitrogen-doped CDs using an MTT assay. Results from this assay suggested that the films containing CDs had only marginally lower cell survival (84.2%) than films without CDs (87.2%) after 3 days of exposure. The authors sought to understand the viability of L929 mouse fibroblast cells after 72 h exposure to CD-embedded cellulose films. Results showed that the viability of fibroblast cells was unaffected in the presence of CDs in the films, indicating that they had no cytotoxicity. The negligible cytotoxicity of films towards mouse fibroblast cells likely stemmed from the negligible cytotoxicity of CDs with cell viability loss of ∼10 − 20 % when cells were treated with CDs at concentrations ranging from 10 to 100 μg/mL [237]. This low level of toxicity at the cellular level not only makes CDs suitable for cell labeling applications but also will pave a new way for their applications in food packaging. In another study, the same group of authors sought to answer whether the nature or matrix of bio-based films could adversely impact the cytotoxic potential of CDs. Sulfur-doped CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal treatment (200 °C for 6 h) of turmeric containing ammonium persulfate. The CDs were then embedded in pectin/gelatin films to investigate their cytotoxicity against mouse fibroblast (L929) cells. The viability of the mouse fibroblast cells after exposure to CDs (1 mg/mL) for 24 h reduced to ∼85% [233]. Min et al. [222] explored the cytotoxicity potential of potato peel-derived CDs at varying concentrations against mouse fibroblast cells. The percentage of viable cells was determined following 72 h incubation. The authors noted that cell viability decreased to 78.8% after 72 h of exposure to 1.0 mg/mL CDs. At the same duration of exposure, the viability decreased to 93.75% at 0.1 mg/mL of CDs. The authors further observed that morphological features of the intact fibroblast cells were not affected by the exposure of CDs at 0.1 mg/mL. However, when cells were exposed for 72 h at >0.25 mg/mL of CDs, the growth and proliferation of the fibroblast cells were negatively affected. The cytotoxicity of CDs most likely arises from their small size, which could easily penetrate into cells and alter cellular metabolism, causing death. Ezati et al. [199] studied the viability of mouse fibroblast cells after exposing them to chitosan/gelatin films containing glucose-derived CDs. The authors observed that these CD-containing chitosan/gelatin films did not inhibit the proliferative capacity of the cells, as the viability remained above 95%. The cytotoxicity of CDs derived from holy basil leaves against human breast cancer cells was evaluated by Kumar et al. [25]. The results showed a cell survival rate of ∼85% after exposure to 200 mg/mL concentration of CDs. The above-mentioned publications indicate that incorporating CDs into packaging films is unlikely to cause cytotoxicity to a level of concern and thus appears to be a safe food packaging application. However, further research is needed to ascertain the cytotoxicity and safe use concentration of CDs for other food applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs in food packaging
ce:section.ce:para: The application of CDs in food packaging is an emerging area of research that offers numerous opportunities to enhance the quality and safety of food products while reducing food waste. Although CDs are currently mainly utilized in non-food applications like optoelectronics and bioimaging, there is a growing interest in expanding their use in food packaging [238]. The application of CDs in food packaging provides several advantages. Firstly, CDs can serve as a barrier, limiting the transmission of oxygen and moisture from the environment to the food. This capability reduces the risks associated with oxidation, caking, and microbial spoilage [85]. The size and distribution of CDs within the packaging matrix can greatly influence their barrier performance. Secondly, CDs can provide additional antimicrobial functions and facilitate the incorporation of bioactive substances with antimicrobial function [134,238]. Furthermore, CDs can alter the release behavior of bioactive molecules by serving as an encapsulating matrix for them [239]. This facilitates the slow and sustained release of bioactive molecules (antioxidant and antimicrobial) into food products, thereby aiding in extending their shelf life [240]. CDs can be utilized not only to improve the properties of food packaging material but also as sensors for real-time monitoring of freshness, temperature history, and detection of pathogens; thus, they serve a dual purpose [238]. The fluorescence changes of CDs can be correlated with specific indicator substances, enabling the acquisition of valuable information on food freshness and safety [241]. The effectiveness or sensitivity of CDs in food packaging applications depends on their functional properties, such as particle size, as well as the characteristics of the food involved. Similarly, the successful application of CDs as sensors to detect pathogens relies on their strong emissive properties [242].

Research to tap the potential of CDs in food packaging has just started, and their application as a multifunctional nanofiller of food packaging materials has been explored. Zhao et al. [243] studied the ability of chitosan films containing banana-derived CDs to improve the stability and shelf life of soy milk. To evaluate the preservative potential of CDs, soybean slurry was prepared using up to 0.2% chitosan and up to 8% CDs, followed by homogenization. Soy milk with 0.16% chitosan and 8% CDs exhibited better physical stability compared to the one without these additives. This formulation effectively prevented the growth of B. subtilis, E. coli, and S. aureus for 4 days of storage at room temperature. The pH of CD-treated soymilk samples slightly decreased, reaching a value of 6.5 due to the weakly acidic nature of CDs. The flavor of CD-containing soy milk was not different from that of untreated control soy milk (Fig. 8a) [243]. Ezati et al. [199] explored the application of CD-containing films to prolong the shelf-life of avocados by coating them with a formulation comprising chitosan, gelatin, and CDs. The authors noted that fungal growth on the surface of avocados was fully arrested when they were coated with CD-embedded chitosan/gelatin-based coating for up to 21 days storage while fungal spoilage was observed after 7 days of storage in uncoated avocados (Fig. 8b). These results speak for potential application of CDs to preserve fresh fruits [199]. The authors applied a coating formulation containing CDs to extend the shelf-life of strawberries and tangerines [182]. Fresh strawberries and tangerines were coated with cellulose solution containing CDs. The uncoated fruits showed visible mold growth on their surface upon 10 days of storage (Fig. 8c and d). Those coated with cellulose/CD formulation did not show mold growth due to the antifungal activity of CDs. The antifungal property of CDs is important for food packaging industries because fungal contamination is pervasive in fresh fruits. Fu et al. [183] proposed beneficial application CDs containing gelatin/chitosan films/coating to preserve fish quality. The overall quality of fish, assessed through total volatile nitrogen levels (TVB-N), showed superior protective effects of these films restricting or preventing spoilage due to microbial proliferation. In addition, coating fish with CD-containing films did not cause an increase in thiobarbituric acid (TBA) levels of fish during storage, indicating that CDs also help slow down lipid oxidation. Given the pH-responsiveness of CDs, they could be potentially used as pH indicators to monitor the spoilage of fish and raw meat by showing clear color changes under UV light. The ability of CDs containing coating to extend the shelf-life of litchi fruits was studied by Mei et al. [213]. The authors produced a coating formulation containing silk sericin, chitosan, and CDs. A thin layer of the coat was created on the surface of litchi fruits, and both color and weight loss were monitored during storage. Preservation of fresh color was recorded to determine the ability of CDs to preserve the color qualitatively. The CD-added coating delayed the occurrence of skin browning in litchi fruits for up to 6 days. The authors also noted that the rate of weight loss of the coated litchi fruits during storage was much lower than the uncoated fruits. The reduced weight loss of coated fruits likely stemmed from the ability of the CD-containing coating to preserve skin integrity and reduce the loss of water through the skin (Fig. 8e) [213]. An illustration presented in Fig. 9 demonstrates the potential of CDs to extend the shelf life of tangerines over a 15-day period. Tangerines were coated with a polymeric solution containing CDs, and their shelf life was compared to uncoated ones. The results showed that the CD-coated tangerines had a longer shelf life, highlighting the ability of CDs to preserve the freshness of perishable fruits for an extended period of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Large-scale production of CDs: Balancing quality, sustainability, and market demand
ce:section.ce:para: To fulfill the large-scale manufacturing of CDs from waste biomass for a broad range of industrial applications, a series of strategic steps must be satisfied. An ideal technique should accurately synthesize CDs from almost every biowaste with large volumes to meet the market demand. Food industries are often concerned about the residual impurities or solvent contamination in the final CDs. Therefore, no matter which technique is used, the quality of the final CDs is likely an important criterion for choosing the suitable method. Another important criterion in choosing the best synthesis method is its capability to convert a broad group of natural carbon precursors ranging from citric acid and glucose to agricultural wastes to CDs. The ecological impacts of the synthesis method should not be overlooked. Industrial manufacturers should be encouraged to use innovative and sustainable practices that contribute to reduction of food and agricultural wastes. Collaborative partnerships with research institutions and industrial experts may assimilate valuable knowledge about their environmental aspects. Direct surface passivation of CDs during synthesis emerges as another crucial aspect for choosing a reliable technique. Incorporating ligands, metal ions and compounds with desirable functional groups during synthesis is highly beneficial in achieving better dispersion, compatibility, and overall performance. Expanding the practical production process from a small-scale laboratory to a large-scale industrial manufacturing is a critical stage that demands extensive collaboration between academics and industrial experts. The use of automated and continuous production without human intervention can ensure uniformity and minimizes the chance of cross-contamination. The adherence of qualities of biomass-based CDs to regulatory standards and established guidelines is vital, especially for food-contact applications. Prior to fully embarking on industrial-scale production of CDs, it will be necessary to undertake thorough market research to fully understand demand and identify potential bottlenecks. To facilitate the lab-to-market transition, running several pilot-scale trials could be an advantageous and robust pipeline to assess the feasibility of obtaining biomass-derived CDs on a large scale. Overall, achieving ambitious production of biomass-based CDs requires a comprehensive plan that entail the convergence of scientific knowledge, engineering expertise, regulatory compliance, and market awareness.

This work systematically reviewed the status of CDs in food packaging and highlighted the latest progress in this field, with specific emphasis on their synthesis from food waste and desirable functional properties that would suit packaging applications. The synthesis of CDs is progressing towards using renewable and underutilized raw materials. Extensive research is being conducted to synthesize CDs from renewable biomass and explore various applications. The remarkable properties of CDs and their significant potential in the food industry are only beginning to be recognized. It has been highlighted throughout this review that CDs can be readily synthesized from various food processing waste and agricultural by-products. The ability to synthesize CDs from living organisms or small organic molecules has opened up numerous possibilities for their extensive utilization in the food industry. Transformation of food waste into CDs can make a valuable contribution to the agricultural economy and help mitigate environmental impact. While the transformation of food waste into environmentally friendly CDs and their application in food preservation offer significant benefits, there are challenges that require greater attention in future research. The emission efficiency of CDs synthesized from food and agricultural wastes is currently low due to a limited understanding of how to enhance their light absorption and emission characteristics. The fluorescence mechanisms of CDs indicate that various factors, including heteroatom dopants, chemical moieties, edge configurations, shape, and size, significantly impact their emission behavior. Incorporating biomass precursors that naturally contain heteroatoms in their structure could partially address the issue of inadequate fluorescence emission. It is important for future studies to focus on tailoring the chemical composition and physical characteristics of CDs, taking advantage of recent advancements in experimental techniques and fundamental science. Overcoming the inadequate emission efficiency of CDs synthesized from food sources necessitates a concerted interdisciplinary effort. Knowledge-sharing and collaboration between a large set of materials scientists, chemists, and physicists may unlock new frontiers and provide entirely new standpoints regarding the green synthesis of CDs. This global collaborative approach could ultimately lead to the design of biomass-derived CDs with superior emissive properties and a broader range of applications in the realm of sustainable packaging. Interest in food spoilage traceability through packaging has started to gain momentum. Therefore, understanding the underlying mechanisms that govern the stimuli-responsive emissive behavior of food-waste-derived CDs becomes imperative. A comprehensive investigation into an intricate interplay of key factors such as heteroatom doping, edge structures, and surface functional groups predominantly yields useful insights into the origins of CD's fluorescence properties. These insights will, in turn, facilitate further improvement in emission efficiency and the fine-tuning of CDs' optical behaviors that cater to specific requirements in packaging applications. The reviewed literature confirms the immense potential of CDs in enhancing the antioxidant, antimicrobial, mechanical, and UV-blocking properties of packaging films. CDs have been shown to be benign or nontoxic in many food applications. However, for their general acceptance in food packaging and food products, a greater understanding of how they enter the human body and get attached to human cells is required. In addition, a thorough and deeper understanding of how CDs interact with the physiological systems of the human body is crucial prior to their integration into food packaging. Exploring aspects such as the distribution of CDs inside the cells, mechanisms of their cellular uptake, and biocompatibility over extended periods is extremely essential to establish a solid basis for guaranteeing consumer well-being and obtaining regulatory endorsement. The purity and stability of CDs are critical for their application in the food sector. The cost associated with their manufacture is yet another obstacle that must be overcome for their application in food packaging. The sustainability and the economic viability of existing CD remain two ongoing problems. Therefore, it is important for future research to aim achieving cost-efficient production methods, possibly by developing methodologies that use abundantly available worthless waste materials, to pave their real-world adoption in food packaging. Moreover, future research priorities should include the application of simple and energy-efficient processes to produce CDs. Recent research on CDs has led to the development of fluorescent inks and LED luminescent materials. The combination of CDs with various bio-based polymers and hydrogel systems presents greater opportunities for the packaging industry to develop flexible films with sensing capabilities, such as fluorescence emission and pH sensitivity. Increased research in this area would help innovate new sustainable packaging materials that can be used to keep food fresh and safe. CD-embedded films have the potential to detect pathogenic bacteria in packaged foods. CDs are also promising candidates for developing fluorescent strips that can detect allergens in food matrices. The research on CDs, especially their application in the food industry, is still in an early stage. Concerted research still needs to be carried out to improve quantum emission yield in CDs derived from a biological source. This review will encourage researchers to undertake further research and explore newer applications relevant to the food industry.

Nazila Oladzadabbasabadi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Software, Writing – original draft. Mohammed Ali Dheyab: Writing – original draft. Abdorreza Mohammadi Nafchi: Writing – review & editing. Mehran Ghasemlou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Elena P. Ivanova: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Benu Adhikari: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Writing – review & editing.
